title: Fitting a piped seam to an upholstery cover
subtitle: Cut and join piping cord in bias-cut fabric casing and stitch it into a seam for a clean upholstered edge
excerpt: How to make and apply piping to an upholstery cover seam. Covers cutting bias strips, encasing the cord, stitching the piped seam flat, and turning corners without puckering.

glossaryTerms.definition: A twisted cotton or polyester cord sold by the metre in sizes 1 to 8 (approx. 3 to 12 mm diameter). No. 3 or No. 4 is the standard for domestic upholstery seams. Pre-shrink cotton cord by soaking in hot water before cutting, cotton cord can shrink 10% and pucker a piped seam if not pre-shrunk.

A strip of fabric cut at 45° to the selvedge. Bias-cut fabric stretches on the diagonal, allowing the strip to curve around corners without puckering. Piping cut on the grain will pull and buckle at every curve.

content.text: Piping gives an upholstered edge its clean defined line and protects the seam from wear at the point of highest contact. The key to flat piping is bias-cut strips and consistent seam allowance, both can be achieved with basic care and a straight rule.
Step 1, Cut bias strips and pre-shrink the cord

Fold the fabric corner to corner so the selvedge aligns with a crosswise edge, this establishes the true 45° bias. Mark and cut strips 35 to 40 mm wide parallel to this fold for No. 3 cord. Join strips end-to-end with a 10 mm straight seam pressed open. Meanwhile, soak the
in hot water for 20 minutes, then hang to dry completely before use.

around the cord with the wrong sides together and the raw edges aligned. Stitch close to the cord using a zipper foot or cording foot set to the left of the needle, the foot rides against the cord and the needle falls in the seam allowance. Do not stitch tight against the cord itself; leave 2 to 3 mm between the stitch line and the cord so the piping can be stitched into the seam without the cord being caught in the final seam.
Step 3, Apply piping to the seam

With the piping raw edge aligned to the raw edge of the cover panel, stitch the piping to the right side of the first panel using the cording foot, stitching just inside the piping stitching line from Step 2. At corners, clip the piping seam allowance to within 2 mm of the cord stitch line, this allows the piping to turn the corner without buckling. Once the piping is attached around all seam lines of panel 1, lay panel 2 right-side-down over it (right sides together) and stitch through all three layers with the piping sandwiched between. The cord stitching from Step 2 is your sewing guide, stitch just inside it.
Step 4, Join and finish cord ends

Where two ends of piping must meet, for example on a fully piped cushion, cut the ends to butt together. Trim the cord so the two cut ends meet exactly, pull the fabric casing back on each end, overlap the casing fabric by 15 mm and fold one raw edge under, then stitch through the overlap. Use the
butt join to set the casing flat and avoid a lump at the join.
